speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: This third interview with Arthur Sundberg is recorded at his home in Potlatch on July 25th, 1975.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Or any other town, but you know, all these little towns was great baseball fans years ago, before the automobiles became too prevalent, you know. And, oh, there was a baseball game every Sunday, and it was men that had jobs and working six days a week and then played baseball on Sunday. But, oh, they used to come up here from as far away as Uniontown with horses and buggies and hacks and all kinds of horse motivated transportation, you know. And that's quite a ways to come, you know, with horses clear from Uniontown. And St. John and Genesee. And they had quite a baseball rivalry going on here for years and years. And, boy, they used to play some pretty hot ball. In fact, the matter is, you know, this town,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: of course, I say this town, it was primarily motivated by Max Williamson. He was a great baseball player, and he was the, he worked in the sales department of the company. And it was through his push and efforts that they built this big gymnasium now. I don't know whether you recognize the building or not, but as you come into town, you make this turn, this great big building on the left there. Well, that was built as a gymnasium, see, for town gymnasium.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, is that the Town and Country store?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah. And Max Williamson, he was a prime mover in getting that built. But he was also the prime mover in getting the American League and National League All-Star team to play baseball here. That's the only small town in the whole Finland empire that has that distinction.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They, you know, we first started contacting them about getting their team in here. Well, no, the town was too small. They couldn't bring their All-Stars in here to play. And he kept Adam, and he got enough back in to guarantee, you know, their expenses.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, oh, as far as, yes, most of us were concerned by, we all thought that was, of course, in our country way of looking at it, you see, about the poorest baseball game we ever saw. Because those guys would get up there and they'd hit that ball. Well, of course, a few of them would go over the fence for a home run, but then most of them would be within the field, inside the field, and there'd always be a man right there to catch it, you know. There was never any errors or anything like that. You take this country ball, boy, it would hit and run and slide and errors and all kinds of excitement, you know. It was really fun to go to a baseball game.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But anyway...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This American National League, that's like the current pro American National League?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yes, it's the same thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they had their All-Star game here for 100 %?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That's right. Well, they was touring the country with their... I see. The American League All-Stars played the National League All-Stars, but on this tour, you see. Uh-huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so these were a lot of the best players in the country?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah, they were the pick of the lot to the entire country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember about when that was?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, that was in 1912.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the game was a disappointment to the people?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, I don't want to say that, but then, you know, we were used to seeing all this excitement, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Scores being made and, you know, errors being made and slide and all this sort of thing. And then, of course, when these dudes get up there, why, whang, one, two, three, and they're down. And then the next side gets up and one, two, three, and they're down. And, you know, that was really professional baseball. That was pretty hot stuff. People see that nowadays, why, that's the hottest thing in the world. But as far as excitement goes, why, it's not exciting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any great players or any, was there one side that local people were for?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, no, I don't think so. You know, there was individuals that... there always has been, as long as there's been baseball, there's been individuals that... Oh, they're all for the St. Louis or they're all for Chicago or they're all for... Of course, they didn't have any teams out here in the West at that time, see. That's right. And, but it was all in the East and in the Midwest, you see. They, you know, out as far as St. Louis and around, I don't remember if the Kansas City had a team then or not, but... But anyway, that was part of the baseball history of this town. Well, how important was baseball to the life of the town, do you think, as far as... Well, everybody, you know, most everybody looked forward to it. And that was a means of recreation, that was just a means of having some sport and, you know, you could think back in those times why... If you had any fun, you made it. You didn't go out and buy your fun like you do now. Everybody got together and made their fun. Well, of course, these baseball games, we had to pay to go in to see the baseball game, you know. But nevertheless, it was just a lot of sport and, of course, these players, they were all strictly amateur, you know. They never got a dime. If they went to another town to play, they got their transportation and they probably got one meal when they were away. But that was the size of it. And, of course, whatever money was taken in at the gate went for equipment. You know, they had to buy baseballs and bats, but each man furnished his own glove or mitt or whatever he used. And another thing, too, if you would see the gloves that they wore them days compared with what they wear now, you'd laugh. You know, nowadays, a catcher's mitt is ordinarily quite small, you know. But those days it was a big rig about like that, you know. But the first baseman, he wore a mitt similar to the mitt that they wear now, except that it wasn't near as big. But it did have webbing between the thumb and the first finger, you know, being the first baseman's glove. But all of the infielders and outfielders, they didn't call it a glove, they called it a tip. And it was just a five-finger glove, no webbing or nothing in fact. Well, if there was any padding in it when they bought it, they'd take it out. It was mostly just in the center part here, it was just two pieces of leather, the inside lining and the outside lining. And they would form their own pocket in there, you know, they'd work on it. But the ball could go right through here, you see, on their glove. Nowadays, all the outfielders got great big webbed, they webbed in all five fingers, you know, just a great big, well, just kind of a basket-like rig, as all the baseball players use nowadays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty competitive to get on the team?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, you bet it was. You bet it wasn't. It was, you take locally, I'm just speaking about our local team here. That's one thing that was at fault with the local team was that they didn't let enough of the young fellas to try out even, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They didn't, unless there was some guy on the team that had a pet friend or that had a boy, you know, you know how it goes. Then, of course, they'd probably let them come down and play catch with them or maybe a little batting practice or something. But anyway, it was just like the old one-horse shay. They all got old together and they all collapsed, you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But when it was going good, it was really a hot team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the guys on this team tended to be veterans who had a lot of experience, middle-aged men?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they got to be middle-aged men, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, there was men playing on the team here, I imagine. Well, I can't say definitely their age, but I just imagine that they were playing here when they was 40 years old, you know. And probably older. And they were still quite active.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But you take as far as relief pitchers is concerned, that was something, unless a pitcher just got...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Being beaten so darn bad that it was ridiculous, well then they'd probably take a man in from the outfield and have him finish pitching the game, see. They didn't have relief pitchers like they have nowadays. A man got in there to pitch the ball, he pitched nine innings of ball. And if it went over time, he pitched 10 or 11 or 12 innings of ball. One man pitched the whole thing. They were pretty tough.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And I'm telling you, they'd throw hard too. There ain't no fooling about that. It wasn't any lollygagging. As far as throwing hard is concerned, they threw just as hard as they do today, if not harder. They really poured in there.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Do they take signals from the catcher like they do in the... Not as much, I don't think. Not in these small towns. Of course, between innings they would talk it over, you know, the catcher. And as they go back to the dugout, they would probably just do something. Generally, the catcher, he'd just catch whatever come. He didn't know whether it was going to be inside or outside or low or high. And it was good baseball.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these games high scoring?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Not necessarily.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: As far as scoring is concerned, they were probably a little higher score than what you see ordinarily nowadays. But they held them pretty close. But there was lots of excitement doing it. I heard that Potlatch had the most, maybe the most outstanding team in the Inland Empire. I'd agree with that. Although, you know, a person can be prejudiced some. But,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: you know, you take Colton and Uniontown, Genesee and Colfax.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And I think as far as baseball is concerned, I don't think Moscow could play really competitive ball against Potlatch at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about these other towns?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they did. They had good, big, husky farmer boys, you know. And then there was, you take, in this town, it was all lumber employed people on the team. But you take in these farm towns, they had mostly fellas that was going to university or college, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think Potlatch was looked at as the team to beat in this era?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I think so. I believe it was. In that particular era, you know, from the time the town started up until, oh, about 19...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: 22, or 20, up to about 24, this was really a baseball town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would people often go with the team to see them play?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, there was, you know, there wasn't too many, on account of the transportation. But, oh, you take, after the First World War, well then there got to be a few Model T Fords and a few 490 Chevrolets. And there was a few of these farmers that had great big case automobiles, as big as this room, and so on, you know. And they would go, most of them. I know, I've driven a part of the team to their games quite a few times, and of course, all roads in the country them days was all dirt roads, you know. You might find a little, just a little short stretch of river gravel on the roads where somebody had graveled out in front of their place. But most of it was dirt roads all over, and when the game was over with, it was just a mad scramble to see who could get out first. Because otherwise, boy, you'd be in a fog of dust, you know, that'd hang there for hours. And we were trying to quite a race trying to get away from there. I know some little towns had rivalries that often ended in fights at the end of the games around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Potlatch like that too? Sometimes pretty tough?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: There was a few among the spectators, I think. I don't think you could say that there was much of that among the players. But the spectators, you know, would get pretty hot under the collar once in a while. But, you know, it wasn't what you might call common. Because you could go to maybe a dozen games and everything was fine, and then maybe one game. Why, there'd be two characters that get, one is pulling for one team and one the other, and one of them is throwing down that this team made an error. And the other one says that the umpire was pulling for this side. You know, all kinds of things. And then, of course, it might wind up being blows, but it wasn't very common.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any idea what made Potlatch have such a good team? You know, why it was outstanding?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, that was, I would give credit for that, just to the motivation of a couple of players. There was Max Williamson, as I said. He was the prime mover. And then Shelton Andrews. He was an outstanding player. In fact, the matter is, he tried out for the Spokane Indians at that time, but he didn't make it. But anyway, he was an awful good player. He played shortstop and he was really fast and he was a good player. And between those two, and of course the efforts of everybody that was on the team, you know, they all were pulled together pretty close, but those two were really the prime movers.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But Max Williamson, I would credit him with being the prime mover.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he play or coach or?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, he played. Yeah, he was a good player, too. He was a big man. To my estimation, he and Babe Ruth were pretty much, they looked pretty much alike. You know, a pretty heavy-built fella, but he was sure good on his feet and a good baseball player.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a power hitter like Ruth?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no. Of course, there wasn't any of them in that category.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Of course, now you take on the other hand, now, Babe Ruth, you know, he's put up here on a pedestal and he's, everybody in this country thinks about baseball, they think about Babe Ruth because of his record. But they don't tell you how many thousand times he got up to bat and fanned out, see. All they tell you about is his home runs he made, see. He'd get up there, maybe get one home run and ten or fifteen times up. Other times it'd be a little infield hit or strikeout or something, you know. And you take Willie Mays and some of these others, they've done the same thing, you know. You know, a lot of the people get the wrong impression, they just figure that when Babe Ruth or Lou Gehrig or some of those fellas got up to bat, it was a home run every time they got up there. That's not true.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it's interesting to hear some about the Potlatch team because they have a reputation, there's no question about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems, do you think the people followed the team's fortunes very closely when they were in town?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, I think the majority of the people did, although there was, you know, there was, as we discussed before, there was ethnic groups here, there was groups of different nationalities. And of course, most of those groups of foreign born people, you know, they weren't baseball oriented, you see. Whatever industry took in baseball, they acquired that after they come over here, you see. Now, I know that there was a lot of the Italians and the Japanese and whatever groups there were that would go to the baseball game because they learned to like it and they would go. But, the prime interest was by people that were born and raised in this country. Of course, for every one of us, in fact, I might say all of us, we had a foreign background someplace, you know. And then we don't want to leave out the Indians either because the Indians were great baseball players. They had their own teams and they would go around and they would play against all of these other towns. And,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: but as a team, I don't believe the Indians couldn't compete too strongly against most of these other teams, but I think that was mostly on account of, they didn't have the team management that these other teams had. But, there was a lot of those Indians that were darn good baseball players.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you say, by the way, these guys didn't get time off from work or anything, to practice.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no. No, you take those days up until the last few years, there was ten hours a day of work and that was six days a week. And then, Sunday morning, they'd go down and have a little batting practice and Sunday afternoon they had a game. And, you know, they, if they knew they had a tough game coming up on Sunday, well then, you take, now they worked till six o 'clock in the evening and by the time they got home and had a bite to eat and change their clothes and so on, it'd be probably seven thirty before they'd get down to the field and then they'd probably get in an hour of practice, you know, before it got dark because they didn't have daylight saving time then, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That daylight saving time didn't come out until the Second World War. But, so you see, now we've got daylight until approximately nine o 'clock in the evening. But then, of course, that's only eight o 'clock standard time. Or, yeah, so you see it'd be getting, start getting dark about eight o 'clock. So they wouldn't have much time after working ten hours a day. This ten hours a day, six days a week, you've mentioned that to me before. Those hours, it's just hard for me to see how a person can work sixty hours a week and have much enthusiasm or much left in him after that, especially if it was hard physical labor. Well, most of it was hard physical labor. But, you grew up with it and you didn't know any different and that's what it was. You just made the best of it, you know. If you got rested up a little bit, well then you'd go out and play just as hard possibly as you worked.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, yeah, in this town, they used to blow a big mill whistle there at five thirty in the morning. That was to wake everybody up.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then they'd blow the whistle at ten minutes till seven. And that meant that if you wasn't already in your place, you'd better be getting there, you know. Because at seven o 'clock when the whistle blew, well then everything was rolling and everybody had to be in their place and working. And there wasn't any smoke breaks or anything like that. They started up at seven in the morning and they shut her down at twelve. And everybody was supposed to be on that job for that entire five hours. Then they would blow their small whistle again at ten minutes to one. And that meant she was supposed to be there. And at one o 'clock they started rolling and she rolled in until six. And that was six days a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they blow the whistle at one or just one at ten?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh no, at one o 'clock they blew the big whistle. See, they had three whistles down there all with a different tone. And the smaller, the one with the smaller chime, that was for this starting up time. And then they had a big whistle that you could hear for, well on a clear day you could hear it for ten miles, you know. And that was for actual starting time. Then they had this other big whistle that was for a fire whistle and that was just like a big foghorn, you know. It had an entirely different tone. And whenever you'd hear those three whistles you knew what each one was, you see. That was the only time they, the only time they... Then, oh, I don't know, long about, long about 19, early 1930s, well then they started blowing the fire whistle every Saturday at noon.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And that was done primarily to see that the whistle was in working order, you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Because, oh, the mechanism that worked this fire whistle, you see, that worked from signal boxes. They had signal boxes, oh, about every, about every two blocks square, possibly three blocks square here in town. They had them, at that time all the power lines run up and down in the street and the signal boxes was on one of the power poles. And then down at the plant they had those spaced through all the departments in the lumberyard area and all over the entire plant. And each, each box had a different number and of course that whistle would blow that number. In other words, if the signal box was 31 they would blow three, you know, three blasts and then a pause and then one. There was no difference in the tone except the, and of course this, this fire whistle, it was operated with mechanism of gears. And, well, it was a good deal similar to what you might see now for a relay, you know, timed relay. And of course the signal come in, that was all electric controlled. But then the whistle was, was steam and sometimes this mechanism, if it got a little cruddy, why, it might rotate around here to where it would open the whistle and it'd blow and there'd stick and it'd just continue to blow until somebody got up there and released it, you see. And then sometimes why the pipe leading to the whistle might get a slug of water, you know, and then it'd make a, just a gurgly sound. And that's the reason that they, the fact of the matter is they, they still have the same whistle and we've got, we've got two fire calls here. One is operated from down at the plant, steam whistle, and then the other is electric siren that's on the fire station.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any fires of consequence that you remember from the early days here?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, not of any, any big consequence. In fact, the matter is they had a volunteer fire department and they had these two wheeled hose carts. You know, the wheels were about five feet in diameter and each cart would carry about 300 feet of two and a half inch hose.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And this cart had a reel underneath the handle where you'd reel in the rope and when they'd take off, well these men would unlatch this reel and grab on to that rope and two men on the handle.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And the mud would probably be an ankle deeper or deeper in the street and here they'd come just running and puffing, pulling them carts, you know. And even with that antiquated fire system, I think in the life of the town, I don't believe there was over three or four houses that burned in the town up until this time. Now then they had a big fire here. They had a big church that burned. That was, oh, I can't remember exactly what year that was, about 19, it was in the early 1940s. And that,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: well, this church had a full basement in it where they had, oh, there probably was seven or eight rooms down in this basement besides a big hall-like room, you know, where they could have dinners and so on. But they, well I don't know, they were doing some work down there. Somebody was doing some cabinet work or carpenter work or something. But anyway, they seemed to think that somebody might have left a cigarette down there and got in the shavings or something. Anyway, this church burned. And then they had a big, this big brick building that had the mercantile store, you know, and the bank and all that. Why, it burned.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But outside of that, of course now when, when this church building burned, why, they even had help from the fire department from Moscow. And the same with when the mercantiles burned. But this church, it was all wood construction and it was just a big, beautiful building.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, gosh, you know, when that burned, why, there was big chunks of burning lumber that sailed, they were here for a mile back in here, you know. The updraft of this heat, you know, that was a big fire.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I want to ask you about the early day working in the mill and around it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me the first job that you had was piling lumber?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, in a box factory. Oh, first job you did was in a box factory.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you then?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, I think about 13, 14 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your job?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Nailing boxes.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Had to be done by hand, you know, with a hatchet. Didn't use hammers, we used a shingler's hatchet, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that was 10 hours a day?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah, yeah. Of course, you know, that was summertime work. And, uh,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: but when I was 15, I got a job working steady. And I worked then in the lumber shed, stacking up lumber and taking it down. And then after that, for, you know, for a while, when I was in the shed there, I was learning to grade lumber. And, uh, well then, this loading cars on the loading dock, it was contract work, what they call it, gyppo. And of course that sounded awful good because they figured, you know, that they'd make a little more money than... So, uh, I went loading cars and, uh, if a person earned their money, they earned it there. We got 15 cents a thousand for loading cars and that had to be split between two men. In other words, you got seven and a half cents a thousand for loading cars.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, those days, railroad cars were small compared with what they are now. Now, a railroad car holds about, approximately 50 ,000 feet of lumber. Well, them days, why the average railroad car held 28 to 30 ,000. Well, if you loaded three carloads a day, that'd be somewhere in the neighborhood of 90 ,000. You probably earned six dollars or somewhere around there. But, uh, boy, you've done a day's work when you handled about 90 ,000 feet of lumber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you do that in a day, yourself?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yes. Yeah. We didn't do that every day. Every day, uh, you see, they'd have a mark, what they call a markup. And, uh, in the shipping office, why, they would, uh, they had what they call a hard sheet. It was a hard paper, you know, that they would write on. And, of course, this order was all typed out on this. And then, of course, the lumber checkers, they would, they would check on this hard sheet, and they would count the number of feet in each load that went in, you know, until they got the total out here. Well, they, they would put the crew's name on the hard sheet, you see. And, as a rule, why, they would, they would, uh, designate two cars for each crew. But then, they got to be quite a bit of hard feelings about it, because the guy in the shipping office, or the foreman, at least, a lot of guys thought so, at least, that, you know, they had pets, you know, they had friends that they would cater to. Well, if you had, uh, two carloads, say, of one by twelve white pine,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: why, that was, that was what everybody wanted, because that would, a lot of scale on that, you see. You'd take three boards at a time and shove them in there. You had a lot of scale for every time you shoved them in. Where, if you got two carloads of one by four, you couldn't hold, and many boards, you would probably hold, well, I would put in five pieces. But that, that was a pretty big handful, where they stacked on top of each other, five pieces. And, but you didn't have the scale in that, that you had in three pieces of one by twelve, see. So, then finally, they'd done away with that system, because, uh, there got to be too much hard feeling. So then it was a scramble, to see, uh, who could get what. Well, then, it got so that some of these favored people, they would go into the shipping office, and they'd look over these hard sheets, you see. And, uh, of course, each one had the railroad car number on it. It would be a Great Northern number, so and so, or an NP, so and so, or Sioux Line, or whatever it happened to be. Well then, these fellas then, of course, there's two men and a crew, and of course, each crew, there's only one man that wore an apron, and that was the fella that was on the inside of the car, because he'd slide this lumber over his apron. As he'd receive it, you see. But, they got pretty foxy, though. They got a second apron, one for each man. Well, they had these, they'd write down these car numbers, you know, and they'd go out in the yard, before the cars were switched, and they'd throw their apron inside the car. Well, as the cars come in on the dock, well, these crews, they was all lined up on the dock, and as the car come in, why, if there was no apron there, they'd throw the apron in. Well, they, ordinarily, they wouldn't know what was going into that car, but there was a few of these characters that knew exactly what was going in, because they knew the car number, you see. And that's just the competition that went on in loading cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not everybody felt that they could just go in and look at that, or most of the time?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, no, that was a restricted area. You know, of course, this shipping office, that was all clerical,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: and about the only time that anybody, other than those that worked there, that went in there, would be if they'd go in to ask some particular question about some particular order, or something, you know. But there was probably two or three crews that were, you know, good buddy buddies, and they'd go in, and here'd be this big stack of orders laying there, and they'd thumb through the whole works, you know, and pick out the ones they wanted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did the, in the scramble, would you just, let's say you were there waiting to see, would you just take whatever come in, or would you look if you were waiting for the car?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You didn't have a chance to look, you didn't see that hard sheet until after you claimed your car, you see. I see, and you didn't know what you were going to get. Uh-uh, no. And you couldn't change once you got it. That's right.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And you take the average mill nowadays, it's amused me here in the last few years that I was down here at the plant. I've heard some of these characters that, you know, superintendents and managers and whatnot, talking. Oh, we had the biggest month in the history of the plant. They probably averaged six carloads a day, you see, that they shipped. But when I worked down there, and after I left there and went to work on the railroad, you know, during World War I time, you see, they averaged 42 to 45 carloads a day out of that plant.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They hauled in 100 carloads of logs a day for a long time.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Of course, now when I say a carload of logs, a carload of logs would scale out around 10 ,000 feet. But that's a million feet a day. That's a lot of timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You could work 10 hours and get your 90 ,000 feet scale? I mean, 90 ,000 feet loaded?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, not always, no. No, no, no. It'd depend a whole lot on what you were loading, you know. And then, of course, there was a lot of time that we were asked to come back for two and a half hours after supper, you see. Now then you'd start at seven in the morning and you would work until 12, and then you'd start at one and you'd work until six, and then you'd come back again at seven and work until 930 at night.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And probably you'd get 100 ,000.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd come back at their request?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, yeah. Because they had orders that they had to get out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get time and a half for that?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh no, there was no such thing as time and a half, double time or nothing like that. That wasn't even heard of until modern times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, at the age of 15, was that a real hard physical strain on you to work with?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, of course, I really didn't put out that much at that age, you know. But then at 15, I worked in the lumber shed stacking up lumber and taking it down, see. And I guess I was about 17, I guess, when I was little in cars.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But it's just like I told you here once before that, oh, kids used to mature a lot earlier than they do now. You take whether it was on a farm or in a lumber mill or mine or anywhere else, you know. You take the average kid, the average boy, you know, when he was 15, 16 years old, he might say just as mature as he ever would be, in all respects.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much money would you be making then, let's say, starting it when you first started when you were 13 and then going up to when you were 17, 18?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you know, they hired water boys, boys that, you know, would carry a water bucket around to the crews. They would hire boys about 12 years old and they would get a dollar and a half a day for 10 hours. But then when you got into production job, regardless of what department you got into, if it was common labor, then you got $2 .25 a day for 10 hours. That was the pay scale. But gyppoing on the loading cars you could make as much as $6 a day. Yeah, yeah, you see they had to, oh, it wasn't too long after, oh I guess probably the plant had run for, oh, probably five or six years before they started any gyppo work.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then of course they, they always, you always had, regardless of what the work is, you've always got some eager beavers, you know. And you've got some men that are outstanding in physical strength and agility and all that sort of thing, stamina. And of course they always feel like that they should be rewarded for all of that over and above some guy that's slow and pokey and all that, you know. So I think that's where the gyppo incentive was born.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And of course you can call it piece work or whatever you want to call it. But it developed into where they gyppoed on the loading dock, they gyppoed in the lumber yard piling, hand piling, green lumber. And they gyppoed in the lumber yard loading out dry lumber, you see. And of course they didn't have any dry kilns until about the time of the First World War. It was I guess along about 1918 or 1919 that they had their first dry kill. And that was, that was something that was just experimenting with because there was nobody in the lumber industry that knew a thing, a particle, about artificial drying, you see. So they put up this big brick building and it was divided into six parts. And of course each one of them was classified as a kiln and they was numbered one through six. But all they had in there was just a mass of steam pipes, coils, you know, underneath. And it was just strictly radiation. They didn't have any way of controlling it or anything else. Well then they did come out then with some charts, you know, that would regulate the heat and the humidity. But it wasn't very successful, although they did dry quite a bit of lumber in there. But they didn't come out with any real scientific dry kilns until about 1925.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then they built 24 big kilns.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But instead of having these heat coils run the full area, why they had the heat coils just in one package here and then they had fans that would blow the heat down through the kilns. Well then, of course, that's all been changed since then, oh, along about 1958,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: 60, somewhere around in there. Well then they come up with more scientific kilns and they used the same building but then they changed their method of drying. So what they use now is that they've got their heat coils running down each side of the kill. And then for a certain period of time the airflow is this way. Cross flow. And then it reverses itself after a period of time and it goes the other direction, you see. Oh, it goes across the back. Yeah. And of course, you know, there's a one-inch sticker between each course of lumber. So then the air on the old kilns they had what they called a chimney that they built right in the stack of lumber. It was wide at the bottom and it would taper up until practically zero at the top, you see, this opening. And the heat would come in here and come up through this chimney and was supposed to radiate out through this lumber. But you would find some of the lumber would be too dry and then other parts of the lumber stack would be too much moisture in it. But the way they dry lumber now, it's pretty uniform.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the defect in the original dry kiln method that they used? Why didn't it work very well?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, because nobody knew anything about drying lumber. They didn't know anything about building a chimney in the stack. And they didn't know anything about the proper location and the amount of heat transfer that they were supposed to have. And it was strictly an experiment and that went on for quite a few years, you know. And it wasn't only this plant, but all the larger plants around the whole west, you might say, including California, they were all working on the same thing, you know, trying to come up with something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of improvement in time could you expect from these earlier dry kilns so far? How long would it take? In the drying time?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah. Well, of course when they used to hand stack lumber in the yard and it was air dried, then that lumber stayed out there for, oh, approximately a year.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, then when they started dry kilning, well, the stack of lumber would go into the kilning. It would probably be in there for anywhere from a week to two weeks. And then it was, the amount of moisture that was removed from the lumber would vary so much, you know, from one board to another. But nowadays I think the average load is about 72 hours. And when I say a load, that means these stacks of lumber are eight foot wide and they're about eight foot high approximately. And of course whatever length of lumber he is, 16 foot average. And they would put six of those loads in one kiln at a time. They would load the whole kiln at once. Where the original dry kilns, they would take out one load today that was supposed to be dry at this end and put in one load of green. It was progressive, you see. And they would move them ahead about one unit each day or each two days, whatever it would take. But now they load the whole kiln and then they empty the whole kiln all at once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did they deal with the unequal drying in the wood? Did they get the wood out and half of it's dry, half of it's partly dry?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Of course then the lumber graders would detect that and then they'd probably have to re-stack it and re-dry it, see. Either that or else if it was surfaced, then they would have to let it air dry. And then of course the customers, they weren't so scientific in their knowledge of lumber either. You know, that they would accept a lot of this partly dry lumber. But I've seen however so much of it. You take a board, say a 12 inch board, white pine, the board is surfaced. You could see these wet spots in it here and there, you know, they just loom right up. But then nobody thought anything up about it because they said, oh well it sits out here for a few days, it'll dry. But they won't put up with that now. Now a customer, he buys his lumber now, he specifies the percentage of moisture. And they have moisture meters now where you take and inspect the lumber. This meter is a hand-held tool with some prongs or probes sticking out here. And all you do is just like that, touch it on the board and it reads right on the dial, the amount of moisture that's in there. You can just touch it here, here and there, you know, and it'll give you the reading of the amount of moisture that's in that board. So they're getting pretty scientific that way. And of course you take as far as lumber is concerned, that's regulated by the architects.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The architects can be kind of a queer bunch as far as that goes. You take molding, you know, you take molding that's used around the house. They had an immense molding department down here. And if you would go into their molding sheds, that is where they had their molding that wasn't sold, you know, stacked up, you would probably find hundreds of patterns.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And as a rule, their lumber salesman that was out in the territory would try to sell molding of a certain pattern. But here's an architect that's drawing plans for a new home, see, or a new office building or whatever it has, see. And he has some cockeyed wild dream that he hatched out and he's got to have this molding, he draws it out here on his drawing board. This is the molding he's got to have. Well, that molding, it might vary from a standard pattern by just a small fraction of an inch in one particular detail of this molding. But he wouldn't accept this standard thing because he's got to have what he drew on this drawing board, see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So what do they do down here?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Molding man has to make a new set of knives and that set of knives has to correspond with this molding that he drew. And of course there's a drawing that's sent in with the order and this drawing is drawn to scale. And when he makes this set of knives, he has to match that thing up, all these little doodads on there, you know, towards absolutely correct. Right, because the lines of his drawing, they can't stick out from this pattern or can't be in from it or anything else that has to be right on that. Well, he's probably ordered a thousand lineal feet or maybe five thousand lineal feet of that pattern. But in running it down there, they would probably, in order to get their grade, the exact and all that, and specified lengths too, you know, they would probably run twice that much while all this overrun goes into the shed. It becomes another pattern that's there and may never be used. Well then, somebody has to try to sell that cockeyed pattern then to somebody else, you see. And that's what you run into.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I want to go back about when you're just a youngster, or the young people working there in the mill, these early years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you worked there from when you were just a young man, the first work that you did, what would you do with the savings, the money that you'd made?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, ordinarily a kid going to work, well his dad took his paycheck, you know, and then he'd dole it out to the kid then, you know, a little each Saturday night or something like that, you know. Maybe you get fifty cents on Saturday night and you go to town. But that's usually the way it went, you know, because there was, up until it all went on, ordinarily, you know, as long as the kid was at home, it generally went into one pot, you know. And of course if a kid got out on his own, or if he was still at home and fully matured, you know, grown up, well then of course he'd say, well, mom, I'll give you so much a month. I'll give you eighteen dollars a month for room and board. And then of course the rest of it went into his pocket, you know. But as far as the young kids are concerned, I think most all of them, their parents took care of that part of it. So a kid might be fully mature in every way, but he didn't have financial independence. That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the maturity that you're talking about at a young age was caused because the kid worked? That it was the work that created this maturity?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, the work and the environment, you know, it was, that's, you know, you take even in girls, you know, gosh, there was girls that were twelve, thirteen years old. Some women would have a baby and so they'd hire this girl to come over and she'd have to do all the housework, you know, wash and cook and make bread and everything because that woman had a baby, you see. And probably the girl was twelve, thirteen years old. But that's the way they used to do. They didn't know any different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were the kids treated as adults besides in the work that they did?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, not exactly, no. Because you take a group of adults that got together and, you know, socially, the kids wasn't included in that group at all. The kids, they had their own group, you see. But, of course, now if they had a group picnic, you know, well then, of course, they were all there. The adults and the kids too, you know, but the kids would have their games over here and the adults would sit over here and drink beer or whatever they were doing, you know. And, uh,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: but as far as the kids' thoughts, you know, his line of thought and his line of action is concerned, I say he was just about as much adult as he ever would be.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, you know, now of course all the kids didn't work that hard, you know. It was, it depended a whole lot on the financial standing of the family, you know. There was a lot of them that still just lallygagged around, pretty much like they do now. But, uh, most of them, most of them worked because, you know, every kid has got, he's got a lot of, oh, desires for personal things. He wants a new suit of clothes or he wants a new pair of shoes or he wants a hat or he wants something that he wants to look as good as that other kid does down there and all that. Well, in order to get it, you probably have to get a job and earn some money to get it.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You take now, oh, the average family that's got kids, they've at least got to buy them a bicycle by the time they're, oh, seven or eight years old. And there's a lot of them that are buying them motorcycles now at seven or eight years old. And if they get to be ten or twelve years old, they're definitely going to have a motorcycle. Well, of course, when I was growing up and, of course, in the particular area where I grew up, you'd probably find one kid in several hundred that had a bicycle and he would be a pretty good sized kid. But he had a bicycle because his dad probably worked in the bank or had a good job or he was a lawyer or doctor or something else, you know. And he had the money and he liked these kids so he bought him a bicycle. But we didn't have bicycles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think you felt when you were a kid it was a big change in your life when you started to work?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah, yeah. There was a tremendous change because I think it's true with everybody because even though your dad took care of your money, you still had more money than you had before you went to work, you see. And, of course, that would give you a little bit more freedom because, you know, you could go down and buy yourself a milkshake or a dish of ice cream where you couldn't before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the idea of self-advancement, of getting ahead, or the idea that young people had of where they were trying to go in life?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It depends a whole lot on the individual and the initiative that the individual has. Now, I don't know whether it would sound, at least I wouldn't want it to sound boastful. But I just went through the eighth grade in school and that was the average for the kids then. You take the number of kids that went to high school, the time I went to school, percentage-wise was a good deal less than those that go to college now. Most of them, when they finished their eighth grade, well then they had their education and they went to work. But, in my case, I don't know, I guess maybe I was more or less born kind of mechanically oriented. But I've always been interested in all kinds, all phases of mechanics, mechanical as well as structural.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I self-educated myself to a big extent because every time I had an opportunity to see something, that is in drawings or in the actual construction of it, that would stick by me. And I'd keep it, I kept it in my head. But in a way, I just naturally fell into the mechanical end of work.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So there was for several years that I'd done all the mechanical and structural drawings for the plant. In other words, I was doing the engineering and I could use a slide rule and all those things and I only went through the eighth grade in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that a lot of kids when they were starting to go to work in those days had high ideals about where they were going to go in life?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: There was a lot of them did. And of course, a lot of them, I think the majority, their ideals more or less developed after they went to work, you know. As time would go by and they'd have a chance to look around and see just what everything was all about, maybe one of them, he wanted to learn to be a draftsman or another one wanted to learn to be an engineer, another one wanted to be a steam engineer. And another one probably wanted to be a farmer, lumber grader. Another one, all he wanted to do was drive automobiles. You know, had a lot of different things that they wanted to do and some of them would follow it up and some of them wouldn't.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, they talk about the rags to riches that I read about. That was a real strong idea a lot of people had that you could become a wealthy man if you applied yourself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that idea, did that idea carry much weight? Did you believe in getting wealthy?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I've never seen any of it in my lifetime, you know, that is where we lived. I can't recall right off of any of them that started out poor and then become wealthy. You know, there were some of them that got to where they made a real comfortable living, you know. But this Thomas Edison stuff, you know, that's something that's very far and few and far between. That isn't anything commonplace about anything like that, you know. What I'm wondering about more is the belief because at least, you know... This rags to riches theory that you're speaking of is, I believe I told you something about what I knew about the Weyerhaeuser, the old man Weyerhaeuser. He come to this country and he had, I believe, six dollars when he landed in New York. And through his skill and manipulation, he got to be a multimillionaire, I guess. But anyway, millions of dollars spread throughout his family. But the theory goes that the man started out with nothing and he'd become a millionaire. And then the second generation, they'd carry it on and build it up still further. But then the third generation is a bunch of squanderers and they'd blow the whole thing. Now that's the theory of rags to riches. But that's true in some cases. Now you take sugar heirs, you know, the Spreckels family, and the Huttons, you know, the Woolworths, five and ten. And some of those, that held very true.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But as far as rags to riches and back to rags again, some of those tried their best to go back to rags, but their fortune was so big they couldn't make it, see?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, whenever you get a fortune so big that you can spend a million dollars a day and still make money, you know, that's simply ridiculous.But that's, in a very few cases, that's just what it was, see?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But like, when you were young, did young people believe that there was a chance for them to become millionaires?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, I don't think that either. Where I grew up, there wasn't any such thought in anybody's mind, you know. I think the average person was just thinking about getting to the point where they could make a comfortable living and get some of the finer things in life. And not everything, but get some of it, and that's what their ambitions were. And I think that it's true right today, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that in those days most people thought of it as material, as that the end of, in life or at work, was materials, material goods, or more satisfaction out of what they did? I think now there's more of a cult or an idea that a person should find satisfaction in his work.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, there is, but I think they're in the minority right now. I think the majority right now is trying to accumulate material things.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You take this operation that's going on down here at Juliaetta, this Carla, what's her name?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Emery. Emery. School for Country Living.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I think that's just one heck of a fine thing right there. But I think that by the people that's living in that area, and for a lot of areas outside there, too, they're just classified as a bunch of hippies and they don't want anything to do with them. They don't want them to come into town and they don't want them to associate with them or anything else because they're a bunch of kooks, you see. But nevertheless, though, whether or not all of these people are going to put all of these things that they teach down there into practice, that's questionable. But it isn't going to hurt them a thing to learn a lot of these basic things in life. Because after all, I think that the sooner that the whole world can get back to basics, I think the better off we're going to be. I don't mean to say that we've got to go out here and dig stumps out by hand or have to go out here and butcher our own meat or anything like that, but nevertheless, the theory of the whole thing, I think, is good. I hope that this woman and her group that's working with her, I just hope that they make a big success of the whole thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it seems to me for you yourself, though, you made money, I mean, living at the job you did, but at the same time it seems like you were doing work that you enjoyed from what you said to me.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, I did. Of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that means you were a lucky one?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I think through my own efforts. I don't give anybody any credit whatsoever of ever giving me anything. Everything I got, I worked for it and I struggled for it and I worked hard for it. And in fact, the matter is, looking back on it, I think that I was being used a whole lot too. But nevertheless, I don't have any regrets because, as I say, we did make a living and of course our goal wasn't sky high. We just wanted to be comfortable and that's what we were. But, you know, if you try to make some comparisons, you go clear off the beam, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Now, when I was doing supervisory work where I had several men working under me and doing a lot of layout work along with it and all that, and I got up to where I got a dollar an hour, I thought, boy, I've got the whole world by the tail. You know down at this plant right now, they're paying common labor, fellas that just go around sweeping up for something, five dollars and some cents an hour. So you can't make any comparison, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And another thing is, you take the way conditions are now, how are you going to build up any incentive?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You can't build any incentive because they're making so darn much money, there's no place for them to go. All they have to do is just go down there and put in eight hours with the, every two hours they get a break for smoke or lunch or whatever they want.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And payday comes, well here's, of course, Uncle Sam takes a big part of that payday. But we've got, we really don't have any grandchildren, but we've got one grandson that's adopted. Our son married a woman that had a boy and of course he adopted the boy, so it's really our grandson. But he was working down here and he was one of these kids that, hey, he didn't care, he'd do anything. They'd tell him to drive a lift truck, okay. If they'd tell him to clean up over here, okay. He'd do anything. He'd work nights, he'd work days, he'd work any time. So he was more or less on the extra board, but they kept him working steady because he'd do anything, see. But he really didn't have any incentive, I never could see where he had any incentive to, you know, try to work at one certain thing.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So consequently, why, he would probably work an eight hour shift and just before the shift was out, they'd come around and say, Ted, how about, you suppose you can work an extra shift, work tonight?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Sure, you don't care. So he'd go home and have a bite and come back and put in an extra shift. Well then of course he'd get off at two thirty that night in the morning, but he'd be back again at seven in the morning for his regular shifts, see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then of course when the weekend come, why, there'd be some watchman, a guard down there that couldn't come, so they'd call up, Ted, how about taking a shift and guard duty?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Fine, okay, I'll be down. In one two week pay period here just a couple of months ago, they pay every two weeks. In that one two week pay period, he had earned over thirteen hundred dollars. Of course he had about six hundred and something take home pay, the rest went to Uncle Sam. But anyway, now that's common labor.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Now where are you going to build up an incentive?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know every day on television here, on channel six, they have a program there from eleven thirty to eleven fifty five, kaleidoscope is what they call it, and they have a conglomerate of everything, discussions and interviews and everything. But there's one part of that program every day is where they read off these jobs that are available through the Washington State Job Bank.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And every day they're looking for skilled laborers, they want painters, they want stenographers, they want bookkeepers, they want lab technicians, and all kinds of things. And you know the average pay is two and a quarter to three and a quarter an hour. And down here in the lumber business your common laborer is five dollars and some cents an hour. And all of the people that they're advertising for are all people with skill. It's not as though every place is paying well, I know that for a fact, it just depends on where you can manage to work. This isn't necessarily the average here. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm wondering when you said that you're used in the work, what do you mean by that? Are you thinking of piling lumber?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, no, no, I meant in compensation.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I know that there was a lot of, I feel that there was a lot of, my superior got full credit for it. And I didn't get any credit for it in particular. And I think that there's just a lot of, just ever so many times that, you know, the work that I actually performed, that I should have drawn a higher scale of pay. But then that didn't fall into the category of customs in the industry. Because if anything at all, why, if there was some pay adjustment they thought was due, they'd probably give it to the other fellow. You know, maybe the superintendent got it. At least he got a pat on the back and they'd have probably a dinner and tell about John Doe, the superintendent there, what he accomplished. Well, he didn't accomplish anything. Somebody was working under him that accomplished all these things, you know, but they didn't get any credit for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the people that lived on Nob Hill here, the brass? Were they, what was their, how did they fit in the community? What was their relation to the rest of the people? Was there really a separate, were they a separate group pretty much?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, there wasn't, not as a group, but they did more or less hang together, but they didn't really have anywhere near the social life that the common people had. People had a lot more fun, I think, socially. But, I think I give you that company paper there where this Laird's daughter was telling about her years here.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I think she was correct in some of those things that she mentioned there, in the idea that her mother kept her finger down on them and wouldn't let her and her sister watch the ball game or they wouldn't let them do this or let them do that. That was true, but that was because they were snobs, you know. Their mother, she was one of these women that she was perfectly willing to isolate herself from everybody just so as to impress people that she was Big Shot's wife, see. In other words, just coop herself up in the house and probably look out the window because I'm the manager's wife. You know, some people might feel where the common people, they were out here on the street visiting and going downtown here and there and, you know, they were living like people should live. But, you take as far as the brass was concerned, you know, they used to have, for a good many years there, the milkman was an independent merchant or milk merchant. You know, he peddled the milk and used glass bottles and put it right on the doorstep. But when it come to paying for that milk, that brass up there was the hardest people in the world to get the money out of, see. And the same with the paper boy. The paper that Spokane Spokesman Review, it cost 70 cents a month for a good many years and you got it 30 days a month. That is, you got it every day. Christmas, every holiday and everything. You got it newspaper every day. And in the winter time, the paper kid would have to slog through snow clear up to his hips because they didn't shovel their sidewalks off. And he'd bring the paper there every day. But when he come to collect for the paper, he'd probably have to go back a dozen times to get that 70 cents. Where you take the common people in town here, why they was happy to pay him every time he'd come, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he explain that kind of difference?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, that kind of difference was just because most of those people up there wasn't making as much money as they'd have people think they were, you see. And they were just living beyond their means. They didn't have 70 cents to pay him, that's all. And that's the reason they'd have him come back. They thought maybe they might get a hold of 70 cents someplace.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But that was true with putting all of them up on the hill up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The superintendent or the big one or two big cheeses, they must have been making good money, don't you think?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, yeah. You take the manager and the assistant manager and the...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I suppose they were up in a pretty high category as far as pay is concerned. But then, of course, when it comes to superintendents, they had superintendents of manufacture and then they had superintendents of shipping. And then they had superintendents of power. And of course, each one of those were on a different pay scale, undoubtedly. And of course, they made good money in comparison with the people that were working under them. But they still didn't make the money that they should have been making to live the way they would like to impress the people that they were living, see. Well, it sounds like a question of class. They felt that they were maybe up for class because of their positions or something like that. Well, that's true. And I don't think that has changed any.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I don't know, you could go back just as far back into history as you'd want to go and I think you'd find the same thing. You know, there's always been a lot of class distinctions and there still is. There always will be, I imagine. You know, as long as we live under this form of government. Of course, if you went to Russia or went to China, well then, you wouldn't have that because they're all the same. They're supposed to be. Maybe. They are, I don't know. Well, they are. You take the premier of Russia. You know, he's the main spring of an awful big country and an awful big population and getting to be a lot of industry, too. He probably makes $400 a month. And that wouldn't even pay for the cigars that one of our executives gets, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, here we pay our president $200 ,000 plus another half a million for expenses and so on. But what do we pay some of the heads of some of our corporations?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They get twice as much as our president gets.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, of course, they're undoubtedly, you might say, earn it. I don't know. But then there's nobody earns what they get, you know. That's an absolute fact. And you take the chairman of the board of some of these corporations. He'll spend half of his time touring around the world with his family. And the other half of the time he spends playing golf. They probably have a board meeting once every three months or six months or maybe once a year and he presides over that board meeting. So what do they do when the board meeting sets is they don't want to raise his salary because they're paying him $254 ,000 a year. So what do they do?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They vote him a big block of stock because he's the one that earned this big profit. And there all he done was played golf and traveled around. Well then the president of that company, he's pretty near as bad. So they vote him a big block of stock. So we've got a good setup, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Somebody told me that they used to have dances. The brass used to have their own dances here occasionally. And that was for them, you know, that they were their affairs.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I can't remember of any of them here. You know, I could be wrong in that. Say for instance now that one of these daughters had a birthday or was going to put on a party and they'd get some music and they'd have a little dance. But that really wasn't, you know, they wouldn't really, you might say, put on a dance just for the brass. They didn't do that. Because, you know, whenever they put on a dance here, now in this big brick building that burned here, this mercantile building, housed the bank and all that. Well then up over the bank they had this big hall. And it was quite an auditorium. In fact the matter is when they built the thing, you know, they built it for all purposes. They even had what we call a nigger heaven, you know, where you could get cheaper seats by going up stairs there, you know. And of course that was blocked off later. But that hall, that had a real good floor. It was all varnished and waxed and everything. And the entire auditorium there was covered by one big canvas. And it was pulled up tight along the edges, you know, through eye hooks.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so whenever they'd have a dance, why they would roll that up, you see, and then they had a real good dance floor. And this place had a huge stage and two dressing rooms. So they would have plays and sometimes it'd be traveling theatrical troupes would come in, you know, and they'd put on a show. And in this hall.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so that's where they held their public dances. But then, oh, after being used for 25 years or so, why, they just, this canvas covering just went to pot and wasn't taken care of. And the hall was being used for more purposes. So then of course they just took it off and it just had the wood floor. And it got a little bit rough then for dancing, but then they still had dances in there occasionally. And they'd put on lots of borax and wax or whatever they used. And they seemed to have a good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you were telling me a little bit about Laird before, once when I was here. And it sounds, what you're saying about him then, that he was more or less like his wife so far as his attitude to young people.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, you take old Bill Deary, who was the first general manager. He's the one that, the main spring, you know, in building this plant. He was a squat, heavy-built man. Typical logger, typical lumberjack. And he talked like a lumberjack and he acted like one. And he'd meet you on, some guy on the street, and he'd say, by God, Bill, how are you doing?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Or something like that, you know. But after he died, and Laird got to be the manager, why, of course people used to see him, he'd go back and forth from his home down to his office, you know. But that's the only time they'd ever get a glimpse of him, you know. And as far as anybody going up to his office, you know, to talk to him about something, well that was just almost unheard of, you know. You had to have a lot of guts, you know, in order to ask for an appointment to talk to him, you know. And, but that was just a difference in the two different people, you know. And you couldn't just ask, just go knocking and... Oh no, no, no. Or ask the secretary to see him, you know. Well you could, but then who in the world had guts enough to do it, you know. You had to have something that was bothering you, pretty bad, something that couldn't be taken care of in any other way. He was out forbidding. Well, I don't know if he ever asked people to be that way, but then that's the impression that he formed and that's just the way people were. And of course, I've seen it, oh gosh, any number of times, you know. He would probably walk from his office and up to the bank because he was always the head of the bank, too, you know. And maybe on the street he had to pass the store, but maybe on the street there, well, there'd be some guy from, you know, he might be from Italy or he might be from Sweden or he might be from some other foreign country, you know. But when he met Laird, he'd tip his hat to the bugger, you know. And that's the only human, the only man in town they ever done that to, but they would do it. And he would nod, you know, he thought that was great stuff, you know. These characters tipping their hat to him.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And it was only just, you know, just a hoot and a holler from his office down to the mill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But he only crossed that railroad track and went down on that plant, oh I'd say about once a year. And that'd be some special occasion where he had, and gosh, when the word got around that Laird was coming down to the plant, well everybody knew there was something up, you know. But that was just the kind of a character he was, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Once a year? I mean, he had that little contact with the...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, he didn't have any contact at all with the working people, absolutely zero.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And you take it as far as his superintendents were concerned, why, they had very little contact with him because their contact back and forth was mostly in paper correspondence, just interpolating, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He was a banker, educated man when he come here. He didn't know any more about lumber than the hog knows about Sunday.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And Bill Deary, he was a man that knew logs and he knew lumbering. He was the one that was put in here as a manager, but then they hired Laird and he was gonna look after the financial end of it. And so Deary, he was only, he was manager here for about eight years. He died, well, about nine years. It started here in 1904 and he died in 1913.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then it was all Laird's.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It was all Laird after that. And then Humiston, he was the, from the beginning here, he looked after the building of the houses and putting up the buildings and so on. And I don't know if he ever had any engineering qualifications, but nevertheless, that's the job he had. Well then, when Laird got to be manager, well then they made Humiston assistant manager. But as assistant manager, he didn't have a thing to do with the plant. His duties was mostly timber holdings and physical parts of the company, you see. But Humiston didn't have anything to do with the plant at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the story that you told me about Laird's talking to the men at that time before an election?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which one was this?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Herbert Hoover. It was when Hoover was elected. And, of course, Hoover was a Republican, and of course, that's what Laird was. So he sent word down to the plant there that he wanted everybody— he wanted to talk to everybody there during the noonhour. Now, you see, he wouldn't donate any company time to talk to these men, but he had to do it on their time, see. So, of course, everybody didn't show up. There were some of them that, you know, went home for their lunch and to heck with it. This was in one of those big sheds. They had a big sorting table running down through the middle of the shed. And the sorting table was up about, oh, about six feet above the floor level.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And this building was 300 feet long. And it was about 100 feet wide. So he got up on this sorting table. So he was about six feet above everybody. So that way, even if you were down too far in the building, you could still see him, you see. And he got up there and reminded everybody that the election was so many days away. And he says, I can tell you one thing. Now, he says, this is a free country and it's a free election. Everybody is given the right to vote as he's fit. But he says, I can tell you one thing. Sure. He said, I forget who he was running against. Oh, it was Smith. Oh, Al Smith. If he's elected, he said, this plant, he said, will be shut down. He says, every one of you will be out of a job the next day. He said, that's a promise. He said, that isn't a threat. He said, that's just a promise. And of course, I don't know how much bearing they had on the election, but I know that there's a lot of them here that voted for Hoover, you know, for fear of that threat. And that was the same thing that carried on throughout the United States. That was part of their election strategy was to threaten people all over, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was very rare, that he spoke to the whole plant?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. I think, oh, I don't know. That's probably the first and only time.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Because he didn't have anything in common with the people down there. You know, if he had any, ordinarily, if he had any message that he wanted to convey to the people down there, he would send it down there in the form of a memo to the superintendent, and he would pass it on to the foreman of the department, and so on, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have friends there?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No. Uh-huh. Not the way we classified.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I thought maybe among the management types, among the representatives.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, well, I know, but then... Let his hair down with it. I know, but then, you know, now, as far as having a social life is concerned, don't get me wrong. He had a social life, but not locally. He maintained a suite of rooms in the Davenport Hotel that was paid for by the year, and he kept his clothes and everything else there, and boy, the old guy, he had a lot of fun. And the same way in San Francisco and Chicago and New York and all over, he would spend most of his time traveling, and every one of these cities, he would keep maintaining a suite of rooms in a big hotel. And the old guy, he had his fun all right enough, but then not locally. He was, you know, this Max Williams in the old town about a while ago that was a prime mover on this baseball in the gymnasium?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He was a fellow that... Everybody liked Max. He didn't have an enemy in the world. He was such an outgoing, open-hearted sort of a guy. Boy, you know, if he just barely knew you and then, say, happened to see you ten years later, boy, this long-lost friend, all that, that was just kind of a character he was.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it was about 1921. He bought himself an automobile, the first one he ever had, and of course, them days, when all the cars were touring cars, had a canvas top that would fold back and lay down. And so, he went up Spokane one day, and by golly, you know, he was about ready to go home, and there was a bakery up there that every time he'd go up Spokane, he used to like to go into this particular bakery and buy some of these hard rolls, you know, and take home. It was just he and his mother that lived up there together. His mother was a widower, and he was single. But anyway, he was just getting ready to go home, so he went in and they bought, I don't know, a couple dozen of these hard rolls.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He got down on the street and he run into a couple of guys that he knew real well, you know, and they insisted, by golly, that he come up to whom and have a drink. Well, Max wasn't a drinking man. I never knew him to ever drink. But anyway, he got tight, and these three characters, they got down on the street and they got playing baseball with these hard rolls.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so, the cop come along and he didn't pinch him, but he just told him to hold it down because it was getting up into the night, you see. And he told him to quieten down and kind of take it easy. But anyway, somewhere or other, the word of that got back down here. And you know, old man Laird, he just come within a fraction of an inch of canon, putting the can of Max over that.
