Because he didn't want anything like that to reflect on any of their upper class employees down here. Of course, Max, he was in the sales department, you see. And so, boy, he just about, he called him into his office and boy, he read the right act to him and he just come within a fraction of tying the can to him just because he had a few drinks and felt good and having fun throwing them biscuits around.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: When Laird... No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he gone a good part of the year from here?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, he'd be gone for, you know, two weeks at a time or a week at a time and sometimes longer. But I can never remember that he ever took his family with him.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, I shouldn't be telling you some of these things on that thing there.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But... Boy, in 1920, early in the spring, in fact, it mattered in February, I'd been working over on the coast and I'd worked for a time in automobile industry over there in Seattle. So I come over here and I went to work in their garage down here selling cars.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And at that time, the company owned half interest in the garage and they sold half interest to the fellow that run it. Well, being as I worked in there and being as I wore fairly decent clothes most of the time, I used to drive Laird's car for him once in a while. It was very seldom that he drove himself and he'd always have a driver. And he had a Hudson Korean car.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so I sent the word, they got the word here at the office, you know, that Mr. Laird was coming home. And we'd been back in the East, some business for the company. And I was supposed to meet him at the Davenport Hotel at a certain hour or a certain day.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So that day come, I was supposed to meet him at, I think at 10 o 'clock in the morning at the Davenport Hotel. So I was up there a little before 10. And so I sit down in the lobby and I thought maybe he was in the lobby, I didn't see him. So I thought, well, he'd probably come through the lobby here. And I waited and waited and waited and so finally went over and I had him paged. So they went around and hollered in his name all over the hotel and couldn't find him. So I waited longer. So about 1230, I went into the coffee shop to have lunch. And to go from the lobby into the coffee shop in the Davenport Hotel, you had to go through a big flower arbor there. They sold flowers, but they had these plants growing in there and everything else. It was just a beautiful place and you had to zig and zag to get through there, you know. So I went into the coffee shop for a lunch and it was about one o 'clock, I guess, when I come out of the coffee shop. And I just started through this flower department there going back into the lobby. I come around the corner and I run face to face with the old man, Larry. And here he had a chick on his arm, you know, just a fine looking dame. Boy, she was really swinging on to him. And he was just getting ready to pay for a great big bouquet of roses, about so big, you know. Here he had this bunch of folding money in his hand, just ready to pay for it. So I just spoke to him so that he'd know that I was there, you know. And I didn't try to embarrass him or nothing. But he got frustrated when he seen me and he didn't know what to do with his money, you know. On one hand or the other, and finally he tucked it into his pocket up here, you know. And he told me, he said, you go into the lobby and wait, he says, I'll be there in 15 minutes. So I went in there and sat for two hours and a half before he showed up, you know. The old bugger, he had his fun all right. You know, I've heard that, well not the same kind of thing, but I've heard that Bill Deary was a, that was probably Bill Deary's biggest problem, because he was a wound chaser. It wasn't any problem, but then it wasn't any secret either, you know. That's the difference between the two. One was all secrets, you see. One could really have his fun where nobody seen him, see. But when he come home, he was just stricter as a devil, see. Then the other one, he had his fun right here, and his whole family did too. His whole family was the same way. I wouldn't say about his wife, but then he had, I think it was three daughters. And boy, they were all wilder than...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But they didn't pull any punches, you know. You're taking the Bill Deary family. Why, they had all the young people from in town was up at their house and having parties and having fun. And they were down here and they were horsing around all over. That was Bill Deary's house. That was just running over with kids all the time, you know, from all over town, see. It didn't make any difference who you were or how you were dressed or anything else up at Bill Deary's house. And boy, you didn't get into old Laird's house, I tell you that. The only way you ever got in there was if you had to go in there and fix something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You drove him around occasionally and took him where he wanted?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, you know, it wasn't steady, but then it was just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he treat you just like a chauffeur?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh no, no, he was real decent. I've got nothing to say about that at all because... But then, you know, he didn't... Well, he didn't break a leg to try to be on the same level with you because everybody knew who he was and treated him accordingly, see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I know one time, in fact, the matter is that... Well, I believe it was the last time I drove him. He probably didn't want me after that. But they was going down in this Clearwater country and look over a bunch of timber. Of course, that was all virgin timber, most of it down in that Clearwater country.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so, I was supposed to pick him up at the office. And the office was this, what's now the town hall, you know, coming in town. And there's a little bit of slope there. And I was supposed to pick him up there at, well, seven o 'clock in the morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then, let's see, who else was it?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, there was somebody else there that I was supposed to pick up with him, I forget who it was. Well, it was one of the stockholders from the east, that's who it was, I remember now. Well, then we were supposed to go up here to Helmer, you know, this side of Bovill, and pick up old Bill Helmer. That's what the village was named after. And he was a company cruiser.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then we cut off there at Helmer and went down through the Potlatch Canyon and up over the ridges, you know, and was going to go to Orofino. But, as I went down here at the office to pick him up, I put the car in neutral and I set the emergency brake, and by garsh, the car just moved right along. And of course, cars them days, you know, had a long stick here for a shift, you know, from the floor. And the emergency brake was down there too, you know, big long lever. And so, I had to sit there and hold the car with my foot. I put it in gear and then held it with a foot brake and I sat there until he come out. And when he come out, I told him about this emergency brake, it wouldn't hold. Well, he always kept his car in the garage downtown there. They kept his car and they were supposed to see to it that it was full of gas and oil and clean and everything else all the time, you see. Oh, well, he said, Art, he said, I tell you, he said, that's my fault. He said, the last time I drove the car, he said, I noticed that. And he says, I forgot to tell Felix, Felix Stapleton was his manager of the garage, about it. But he said, when we come back, he said, we'll have it fixed. So, knowing that the emergency brake wasn't any good, of course, I didn't get out and try to fix it, did I?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He had a good service brake, a foot brake, they didn't have hydraulic brakes like they got now or any power brakes, but just foot power. But we made it fine and dandy up the line to Helmer and then went down through Park and, you know, down the canyon and going up the other side of the canyon. And then, of course, it was just narrow dirt roads, you know, just wide enough for one rig to drive on. But the road was rough, you know, in places, you know, rocky and so on. And after, now the brakes were fine going down into the canyon, but then going up the other side, it didn't have any occasion to use a brake.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so, we got up on top and the road was just winding around through the timber and they come around a bend, sharp bend in the road and here was a dog gone tree laying right across the road. And it was, oh, it was a tree that must have been two feet or better in diameter, you know. Well, I slammed on the brake and pulled up on the emergency brake automatically, but it wasn't doing a good, but the car just kept on going. It never made any attempt to slow down at all. Well, the way the cars were built them days, you know, the frame used to extend out in front of the radiator, you know, for a foot and a half or so and that's where the springs would fasten on. So, you know, your front springs was always out there a foot and a half or so in front of the radiator. Well, this frame, you know, hit this tree and the car went up in the air and the front wheels went over the tree and when it stopped, one hind wheel was behind the tree and the other hind wheel was on top of the tree because the tree was a little bit slant in, you know. Well, the first thing that come to my mind was, was anybody hurt, see?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so the top on this car, you know, it had these wooden bows that supported the top and, of course, they stuck down there an inch and a half or something, you know, below this top. And, of course, these two characters layered in this stockholder, they were in the back seat and old Bill Helmer and I was in the front seat. But they flew up and old Laird he cracked his nose, the bridge of his nose on the bow there, you know, to where he was bleeding a little bit there. But that was all the damage was done there. Well, here he was, a hundred miles from nowhere and the car was on top of this tree and, but it just happened that Bill Helmer, he never went anywhere without a cruiser's axe. If he went in to sit down to eat dinner, well, he'd have his cruiser's axe with him. And, of course, you know, it had a little handle on it about that long. So he started in and he started chopping on that tree and he cut that tree off on both sides of the car.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And while he was doing that, I was looking the car over to see what damage was done. Well, the frame that extended out in front there, it was buckled under some, you know, and the front wheels were popped around. So I crawled underneath the car to see what was wrong and here these brake rods, they were coupled together with a turnbuckle. You know, that you could adjust your brake pedal by adjusting that turnbuckle while evidently these lock nuts weren't locked tight enough. They had a lock nut on both ends of the turnbuckle, you know, but they wasn't locked tight enough. So in driving up over this, out of this canyon and so on, well, this thing jiggled and jiggled and loosened up, see. That's the reason I didn't have any brakes. Well, I screwed that back together again and we pried and jacked and lifted and grunted and got the car up over that piece of log to roll that piece of log out of the way. Well, then we got in and then we, of course, we'd lost two or three hours there, you know, getting this car off of that log. So then we started up and got in the car and just a little ways ahead of us was another turn to the left. And by golly, you couldn't get the car around that turn, see, because the steering mechanism is all goofed up on it. So I'd get partway around and I'd have to back up and then, but that's what I'd have to do on every left turn. It didn't go too bad turning to the right. And, well, then from on top of the mountain and going down into Asaka, that was a long grade. I'll tell you, that was, I don't know, seven miles or something like that. It was a long grade. And there was only two or three places in that entire road there where there was a turnout where you could pass anybody, you see. They had a hole dug in the side of the hill where you could pull in if you heard somebody coming. But we made it down there, but it was pitch dark before we got into Asaka.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And we was way up on this canyon rim, you know, and you could look way down in that canyon and see a few little lights glimmering down in there, you know. Well, you know, these fellas had never been over that road. That old Helmer had, you know, but Laird hadn't and neither had this other guy. And if they'd had any idea, if it had been daylight when we went down, I know they'd have walked the whole darn distance because that was a bad road and the way the car was goofed up, you know. But anyway, we finally made it into Orofino that evening, that night. Oh, I don't know, it was 8 .30, 9 o 'clock or 9 .30 or something by the time we got into Orofino. By that time, I was darn tired that I didn't care whether school kept or not. So we went to the restaurant and had our supper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't care whether what kept?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: School kept or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: School kept?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That's just the saying.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's happening?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you don't care which way it goes, one way or the other, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: School?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, that's just a hillbilly saying, you know, that he didn't care whether school kept or not, see. That is, you could go to school or not. But anyway, we had rooms at the hotel there in Orofino and of course we were supposed to, the trip was planned to be gone for a week or two, you know. Traveling, cruising all over there, looking timber over, you see. But then the next morning, why instead of me going on that cruise, why I had to take the car to Spokane and get it fixed. And then they rented the car to go on their journey, you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So, going from Orofino, I come across the hill to Kendrick.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then from Kendrick to Troy, well, going from Orofino up over the hill and then down into Kendrick, it was just like this, you know. And then going down, why you could see your own tail light, you know, as you went around, some of these hairpin turns, you know, and then you could look right down. It was just like looking down a chimney. And there was this car all goofed up, you know, and every turn I'd come to to the left, I'd have to make maybe two or three times, back up two or three times to get around the turn.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so I finally made it back in one day from Orofino back to Potlatch. Well, then the next morning then I was going to go from here to Spokane.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, Harley Decker, he was just getting ready to marry Laird's daughter. And this Harley Decker was a heck of a nice fella. So he found out I was going to Spokane so he wanted to know he'd ride up with me because he had to go up and buy himself some clothes for getting married. So I said fine. I was glad to have him come. Well, I drove that car up to Spokane and there I had to stay at the Davenport Hotel for a week while they was fixing that car. A short, tough duty.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: When you were out fixing that car on the road getting it off of that log, I imagine that Laird and the stockholder just looked down and watched you. Oh, they just went over to the side and they were both concerned but there wasn't anything that, you know, neither one of them knew anything about work. And of course they got a pry hole under it and we kind of leaned on it. There was a little leverage but they helped a little bit but it wasn't too much help. And then when this tree went down it broke a telephone line down. It fell down across the telephone line. And so I cut off a chunk of that telephone wire and I had to jack the front end of the car up because the shackles were broke, you see. And I got a chunk of wood and put in between the frame and the axle so as to keep the fenders off the tires and then had to wire that all in there with some of this wire, telephone wire, you know. It was sure a haywire job, I'll tell you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, talking about Laird, what about Deary? Was he, I heard him call the self-made man. He was a man that really got a long ways on really, you know, just on...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Jawbone. I think that's true, although there wasn't any of us, well, you know, the ordinary people here that knew him before he come here. Although, I guess maybe there were some of the older fellas that was in the logging end of it that knew him from back in the Midwest, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But from what we always heard was that he never had any particular education, only just a common education. But the reason that he got up in his position was just the drive that he had. He had a tremendous amount of drive to him.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I think if Mountain was in his way, he'd move that mountain. He was just that kind of a guy, you know. And he was built that way too. He was built just like a little stud horse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was Irish, right?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, that's what we all figured. Of course, I don't know of anybody that I knew of, and asking what particular nationality he come from, but we all took him to be. And I think that Deary, I think the name of Deary, I think has a pretty strong bearing on Ireland. Was he, I've heard that he was a pretty tough guy, and that he had to be, I mean, as far as willing to fight. Well, he never had to resort to anything like that after he come out to this part of the country. He might have had that in his earlier days, you know. Because, you know, on his way up, because undoubtedly he probably had a pretty rough road to hoe on his way up to the top. I wouldn't say that he done anything wrong or anything like that, but in that particular era, the way you got up there was just through hard work, and then sometimes your fist would help a little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that when he was out here, that he really, did he have much to do with the men as far as work a day?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, I wouldn't think so. Although, if he met a person any place, you know, he'd speak. But, you know, after he got out here, his job, you know, kept him pretty well in the office. Although, you take it the, at the time that he was the manager, the office was down at the plant. They didn't build this office uptown here until 1916, and he died in 1913. So, down at the plant, they had a big, long clapboard building, you know, with 12-inch boards going up and down, and then a 1x4 batten on the cracks, you know. And the inside was whitewashed, and the outside didn't have nothing on it, just boards. And that was their office, and of course, he had a little private cubicle in this office for himself, and then the rest of it was space for bookkeepers and everything else. And, of course, those days, all the bookkeepers would work at a desk, you know, that stood up about this high, and they all had to stand up and do their bookwork, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just a little table they stood at?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, it had a sloping top on it. Yeah. And it was generally a pretty big table, but if you were a bookkeeper, you stood up all day long, and all the bookkeeping was done with pen and ink. And they used to have some pretty fancy scribes those days. Boy, some of that scrollwork was really something to look at, you know, and the penmanship was really fancy. I heard that Bill Deary didn't know much at all about business and figures and that sort of thing. Well, that's the reason they brought Laird in. He was a man who was educated for that end of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Bill Deary, though, did he set the lumbering policies? I mean, was that all his?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no. It was a big corporation, and of course they had sales departments in Minneapolis, and, you know, all their sales went through the sales department. And of course they had their salesmen that traveled all over the country, you know, different territories, and selling their lumber. And of course whatever orders come in, why, they would try to produce that, you see. But their way of making lumber, their way of grading lumber, and selling and all that was so far...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: This was strictly, you might say, a white pine mill, although they did cut quite a lot of fir and larch. They made red fir and larch dimension, and then of course they cut an awful lot of vertical grain larch for flooring, you know. And that was put near like a hardwood floor, and that was all run into four-inch tongue and groove flooring. But the bulk of their lumber was white pine, and they cut a lot of yellow pine, too. But it was, you might say, a white pine mill. Well, they would cut... A big percentage of their output was one by twelve boards, you see. And of course they were dried out in the open, air dried, and they were brought in and run through the planer. Well, in air drying, there's a lot of these boards, the tension in the board would make the board cup a little bit, you see. Well, the tension then in your planer, because you've got the bottom rolls and the top rolls, you know, that feeds the lumber through. You've got their set, you know, if the board is, say, one inch thick, well, these rolls are set down to about a half inch apart. And heavy springs on them so that when the board come in, this top roll would jump up a little bit and, you know, keep enough pressure on it. Well, if there was a little cup in that board, why then it would probably develop a little crack and split in the end. It would probably be split for two feet or three feet or four feet, you know, on the end of this board. Well, ordinarily if that board wasn't split, that board would probably be a number one common. Of course, nowadays they've got all kinds of other names for their lumber, but then it used to be graded as number one, two or three common, number four boards. And then there was D-Select and C-Select lumber, you know, that was just select boards with no knots, no blemishes in it. Well, this one by twelve board that had this crack in the end, well, just because it had a crack there, instead of being the number one board, it was graded down to a number three board. And it was sold that way. Well, the purchaser of that board, he could rip that board and get a lot of, probably a lot of select trim for inside of his house, you see. Well, they found out a good many years after that, when they got some of their college educated people in the industry, they got to looking at it in hard money. And they said, well now,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: a number, a C-Select board, one by four, four inches wide, twelve feet long, is worth so much in money. So many cents, or whatever it might be. Where this one by twelve board, sixteen feet long, in a number three common with this crack in it, it's worth so many cents, so it's worth less money than this four inch board, twelve feet long, you see. So what did they do?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They started in ripping and trimming and ripping and trimming and all this stuff, instead of this natural resource going to somebody that can use it, there's two thirds of that board probably would wind up in the hog and just chop it up for hog fuel and go to the boilers and burn it up for fuel, see. You get what I mean?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You see, if you were to buy that board, you could cut off, here at the end of this crack, you could cut it off so you'd have a twelve inch board, say maybe ten feet long. And then this other one here, you could rip that by hand and get maybe a six inch board out of it and a four inch board, and use that for trim someplace, around a window or around a door or something, see. But after they started getting smart, then you didn't get that good easy, because all this other, the part that wasn't profitable went to the hog.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It took a long time for me to get that in my head to see the sense in it, and I don't, right to this day, I don't see the sense in it. But nevertheless, that's the way lumber business is run.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that in the early days of Potlatch that there was a much of a return on their investment? I think about the tremendous amount of money that had to go into this just to create a town here for people to live, and the good deal that you told me that people got, pretty much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, with all that huge capital outlay and the lack of some of these refined methods of getting every nickel out of the board, did they do all that well here?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you've taken off for quite a few years there in the beginning.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: As I said a while ago there, that they probably manufactured in the neighborhood of a million feet a day.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And probably the average price that they received for their lumber was somewhere around $25 to $30 a thousand.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And I think the thing that brought the average up that high was all this pattern stock that I told you about that I think before. That was three and four inches thick, and it had to be clear white pine.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And in these foundries they used that for making patterns for foundries, you see. And so that brought a pretty high price. And then they got a good price for match stock. Match stock was four inches thick white pine, and of course that had to be very few knots in that because that was all cut up in little blocks and split for matches, you see. And that brought a pretty good price. Well then of course their method of logging would be that they would make logs up. Oh, they wouldn't quit at the first branches, but then they didn't take the tops. They got down to 10, 12 inches in diameter, and they left that in the woods, you see. So they got a lot of select lumber, that is, lumber without any knots in it, you know, and that brought a good price. But the average lumber that they sold was probably, oh, $15 to $18 a thousand.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So it's a far cry from what it is now when you're paying $250, $300, $400 a thousand for it, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And of course the reason that these so-called lumber barons become millionaires, you know, in the early days was because they got so doggone much timber for nothing, or practically for nothing, you know. See the railroads, the government granted the railroads every other section.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And you take the Burlington Northern and the Milwaukee, they still own an awful lot of timber right to this day. After selling timber for 60 or 70 years, they still own a lot of it, so you can see how much timber land that they were given for nothing. And Weyerhaeuser too, when they came out here, got this... Well, they bought, you know, they supposedly bought timber, but then, gosh, when you say bought, you know, you could give some guy $10 and you bought it, didn't you?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, you know, prices today, that same tract of timber would be worth probably in the millions. But, so you see, they could hire a lot of people and still sell their lumber at what we now think is a very low figure, and still make millions of dollars. But, of course, what we consider now is a low figure wasn't by any means a low figure at that time. You told me that the first time I was here that old man Weyerhaeuser actually stopped and talked to you. You bet he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me how that happened?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, as I say, the stockholders used to come in about once a year and have special trains. They would have all 10 or 11 great big mainline railroad cars, sleepers and diners, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And all staffed with a bunch of niggers and white, all dressed in white, and boy, quite a show for the natives here when that special train come in. And, of course, they would, this train, all of the stockholders would, you know, would ride this train.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd come all the way from the east on it?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, they'd come from Chicago. You know, I think it had started Chicago, and of course these stockholders lived in different parts of the United States, but they would congregate there and get on, and they'd come through. And, of course, wherever they had a holding, why, they would stop. But, of course, this one here was the big deal for the warehouses. This was the biggest thing they had and the showpiece of the whole warehouser organization. Well, when they'd get here, of course, now, they didn't just jump off the train and come right on the plant. So, you know, it took a little while to get there. They'd probably have a meeting together, you know, with some of the local officials. But then, they always had to make a tour of the plant, and they wanted to see everything, you know. And, of course, there was a lot of these stockholders that had their wives with them, you know. It was a family affair. But, this whole thing was just a big bore to the old man warehouser. You know, it used to bore him to death. And, these people, you know, they were bankers and they were millionaires and they were all kinds of people. And, although he, in some respects, fit in the same category, but by nature, it didn't, you know. And so, they'd all come like a herd of sheep, you know, into this department and look this all over and then they'd go into another department. And then, sometimes, they'd break off in different little groups, you know, and they'd go here and there. And then, after a while, they'd get back together. But, the old man warehouser, every time he'd come, he'd take off by himself. You know, he didn't go with that bunch at all. And, you know, he wanted to see the plant, but he also wanted to talk to the people. And, wherever, whenever he, in any department that he was come in, that wasn't too noisy, you know, any place where he could talk or be heard, he used to like to stop and talk. He didn't care. He could talk with one of these Italians or anybody else, just as easy as anybody. And, that is, if they'd talk to him, he'd talk to them. And, he was a nice old fella. And, I was working down in the lumber shed down there, stacking up lumber. And, this old guy come in the door, they had big sliding doors on both ends of the alley there, you know. And, they had two sets of these narrow gauge railroad tracks down in each shed.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, you'd have low lumber on there, you'd, you know, taken it down and stacking it in different places, wherever the grade mark called for, you know. And, you always had a couple of saw horses there, where you'd, you know, lay lumber down on when you're grading it. And, so this old guy come in there and looked around and kind of looked at the lumber.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He said, hello. He said, my name is Weyerhaeuser.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, so, kind of took a minute or two for me to soak in, you know, as to who he was. Although, I knew, you know, that the train was in, you know. And, I knew that this group was here. And, so, he just started visiting, just casually, just like anybody else would, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just with you alone?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah. And, so,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: of course, when he was visiting, why, he just sat down on this saw horse and sat there and looked around and talked.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He said, sit down. So, I sat down. Yeah, he visited there for, oh, I don't know, five, ten minutes or so. And, then he got up and wandered off to someplace else again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he talk to you about?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, just nothing in particular. Just about the lumber and the job. How I like my job. I can't remember what he talked about, but it's just, you know, just talk. But, you take all the rest of those characters, in that whole train load. I don't know of any of them that even nod to anybody. Because they had to come and see what they owned, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he was even interested in what he thought? I mean, did you talk to him at all?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I don't think so. I don't think he looked at it that way. He was just, you know, friendly, that was all. He didn't care what I thought about warehouses or about anything else. I don't think it even entered his mind. Now, you take his sons. Now, you were talking about rags to riches a while ago. This old man warehouser's sons, he didn't baby them one bit, I'll tell you. I really didn't know them. I'd seen them several times on these stockholders' tours. But I worked with a man down here, old Charlie Parker was his name. And he had worked for the Weyerhaeuser in one of their mills back in the Midwest.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And you take the, at the time that Parker was working here, well then, this old man warehouser's son, he was the old president of the company at that time. But when Parker worked in that mill back there, this fellow that was president of the company, he was the millwright in the sawmill. Because the old man warehouser, his sons, they had to learn this work from the beginning, right from the bottom up. And they worked in the grease and they worked, they repaired the machinery and they'd done everything. They didn't pull no punches with them. But then when the third generation come around, well then they got to be bankers. Because they'd go to Harvard or they'd go to Yale or somewhere back there, you know. And they got an education where the old man Weyerhaeuser' sons, they didn't have any of that formal education. They had just, I think, if I'm not badly mistaken, I think they just had common school education and then they went to work in the mills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they successful, his sons?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, you bet they were. They were part of the machinery that built the thing up to the empire that it finally developed. Of course, you know, it's spread out now in such a conglomerate that you take Potlatch Forest, Potlatch Corporation as they call it now, started out as Potlatch Lumber Company. Well then, you take in the early 30s, well in the latter part of the 20s, they started building that mill at Lewiston.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so one of the young Weyerhaeuser, he was the head of that plant down there.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But what all the information we could get was that he got that in such a financial bind down there that they was about to lose the whole thing. And then, Rutledge Mill in Coeur d 'Alene, that was strictly a Potlatch Corporation layout. But then, of course, each outfit was run as an individual corporation. Well, it was out of timber. They had the mill, but they was out of timber. Potlatch had the mill and Potlatch had the timber. Lewiston had all this clear water timber. They had lots of timber. So, the story goes that we got at least, whether it's true or not, I wouldn't say that it's true, but in a way, the information we got was that in order to save that plant at Lewiston, they incorporated all three mills into one outfit. Potlatch, Lewiston, and Coeur d 'Alene, and made it Potlatch Forest. But now then, they've changed the name to Potlatch Corporation. But, since that time, they've had half a dozen presidents of the company and chairman of the board. None of them have ever been a warehouser. They've all been men that have been brought in from other industries, you know, from the chemical industry, the oil industry, or some other industry, who've been brought in as a head. But, they were brought in on account of their management ability. So now, Potlatch Corporation, they own several plants in California, Florida, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Minnesota. They own the whole state of Arkansas. And, so they're scattered over the entire United States, see. But, it's still classified as a warehouser operation, see. But, Weyerhaeuser, of course, was the parent corporation, you see. But, since then, you know, you've got Potlatch Forest, and then you get over on the coast and you've got, you've got warehouser, Longview, and over, it's in the Snoqualmie Falls. And, they're all, but nevertheless, these stockholders, you know, they're all, you know, they all own stock in each one, or maybe just one. So, it's just a big financial deal now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that's, if you look back at it, you know, in retrospect, and I know it's hindsight, but does it seem like that the way that this kind of development of a corporation with the stockholders and all that is the best way to distribute the, to use the resources, like of the timber, of the forest?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, all I can say is, most of us figure that the United States is the foremost and the outstanding country in the world, financially and mechanically, and production-wise, and everything else. Don't we figure it that way, most of us? Well, how did it get that way?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It got that way just because a man like old man warehouser, come in this country with six dollars in his pocket, and through, start out with a partnership, and then branched out into selling stock. They need a million dollars, so what do they do?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They sell a bunch of stock. Well, then, here's some guy that owns an oil well, or, or, maybe he's got a flour mill, but he's got a hundred thousand dollars that he puts into this, buys stock, see?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So, he's a big stockholder. Well, then, another one, he's got fifty thousand dollars, another one's got two hundred thousand, but anyway, that's the way the kettle boils, where you take, in some of these other countries around the world, it's a family affair. It isn't a corporate affair, you see. You take, if there's a family that's got a foundry, they make castings, see? And, then, of course, as the sons grow up, why, they're brought into the, here to learn this business, but it's management end of it, you see?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, all these people in the village, or in the town, where this foundry is, not a single soul owns any stock in that, because that's a closed deal. That's just a family deal. It's different in the United States. The United States, you can go out here and start up a garage to repair automobiles. But, you've got to have ten thousand dollars here for equipment and for a lease on this building. So, you come around and say, by golly art, I need ten thousand dollars to get going. Well, I know that you've got the ability to run the thing, so I've got a thousand dollars I'll loan you. And, somebody else will loan you a thousand dollars. So, you get going on that thing. That's the way they do it in this country. Well, that kind of scale, I can see it, makes it, you know, that's real clear to me. But, it's when you get up to the, you know, the big stockholders and you describe these people coming in from Chicago who don't know a thing about lumbering, a lot of them don't care at all about the town, about the, really about the people or anything. And, they're the ones because of, you know, the money makes money. But, the people who work in it, like, you know, the average people in Potlatch, they're probably not going to be stockholders in there. Or, don't have the chance to become stockholders in the country. Without those, without them people back there, without their money, we wouldn't have a job here, see. Their money created jobs.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They don't know anything about the lumber industry, lumber production. People here know a lot about lumber production and how to make lumber and how to load it and ship it and that sort of thing, see. But, those people back there, they know how to sell it and how to get the money out of it. And, even those that don't even know that, without their money, this thing here could never have been built. It couldn't have been possible. Well, Weyerhaeuser didn't have the money. The Weyerhaeusers themselves, they couldn't have built this plant. They had to have money from other people to help them build this plant, see. And, that's just the way the whole country is run.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, you can take a look at the financial section of the paper every day and here's General Motors. Stock is selling for so many dollars and cents a share. Here's Ford and Chrysler and here's Weyerhaeuser and here's this chemical outfit and that chemical outfit and they're all listed. Here's a water power company and power and light and all that. They're all listed there. Well, you've got a little money in the poke and you think, well, maybe I can make a little money on it so you buy some of that stock. Well, if you've got some stock in one of those corporations, then you're entitled to sit at their stockholders meeting, see, because you're a stockholder. You might only have $500 in there, but still, when they have a stockholders meeting, well then, of course, when it comes to both...
